children.name: Esox
children.description: Pike and muskellunge — the apex freshwater predators of the Northern Hemisphere, torpedo-shaped ambush hunters with a flattened, duck-like bill lined with hundreds of backward-pointing teeth. The northern pike (gädda) is the dominant large predator in virtually every Swedish lake and river. Pike are solitary and fiercely territorial, lying motionless in weed beds before lunging at prey with explosive acceleration. They will take fish more than half their own body length, and occasionally ducks, muskrats, and even small otters. In Sweden, pike fishing is a national passion — the spring pike season opening is a social event rivalling the crayfish season. The largest pike are old females; females grow much larger than males throughout their lives.

children.name: Esox lucius
children.description: Sweden's most iconic freshwater sport fish (gädda). Present in virtually every Swedish lake and slow river. Can reach 25 kg and over a metre in length. A keystone predator whose presence structures entire lake ecosystems. The female is substantially larger than the male; fish over 10 kg are almost invariably female.

children.name: Esox masquinongy
children.description: The largest member of the family — the legendary 'muskie' of North American sport fishing. Can exceed 1.8 m and 30 kg. Known as 'the fish of ten thousand casts' for its elusiveness. Hybridises with northern pike to produce the fertile 'tiger muskie'.

children.name: Esox reichertii
children.description: The largest and most distinctive pike, native to the Amur River basin of China and Russia. Covered with small, regular dark spots like a leopard. Can reach 115 cm. Its range does not overlap with northern pike, making it the apex freshwater predator of the Amur.

children.name: Esox niger
children.description: The largest of the pickerels, named for the distinctive chain-link or reticulated pattern of dark markings on its gold-green flanks — one of the most beautifully patterned freshwater fish in eastern North America. Found from Nova Scotia to Florida and west to the Mississippi drainage, favouring heavily weeded ponds, lakes and sluggish streams where it ambushes prey from cover in classic pike fashion. A popular winter ice-fishing target throughout New England, the chain pickerel is more cold-tolerant and active in frigid temperatures than most co-occurring predators, giving it a competitive advantage in early spring. Can reach 75 cm and about 3 kg.

children.name: Esox americanus
children.description: The smallest member of the pike family, rarely exceeding 35 cm, found in heavily vegetated, slow-moving or still waters of eastern North America. Two subspecies are recognised: the redfin pickerel (*E. a. americanus*) of the Atlantic coastal plain, with vivid orange-red fins, and the grass pickerel (*E. a. vermiculatus*) of the interior, with dusky fins and a more westerly distribution. Despite its small size it is a classic ambush predator, lying motionless in aquatic vegetation before striking with the speed and aggression characteristic of the genus. An overlooked but charming miniature version of its more famous relatives.

children.name: Esox cisalpinus
children.description: A cryptic species described in 2011, long overlooked within the northern pike because of their similar body form. Endemic to the Po Valley of northern Italy and the Ticino canton of Switzerland, where it inhabits slow, weedy rivers and floodplain lakes. Distinguished from *E. lucius* by its darker, more heavily pigmented flanks, a different scale pattern around the cheek and operculum, and significant genetic divergence. Its restricted range and the ongoing hybridisation with introduced northern pike — planted widely for sport fishing throughout its native waters — make it one of Europe's more imperilled freshwater fish, though it received little conservation attention until its species status was formally recognised.

children.name: Esox aquitanicus
children.description: Described as a new species in 2014 from rivers of southwestern France — the Garonne, Dordogne, Charente and Adour drainages of the historic Aquitaine region. Molecular phylogenetic work revealed that pike populations in this Atlantic-draining region are genetically distinct from both the northern pike and the southern pike, representing a third European lineage with a long independent evolutionary history on the Iberian-Aquitaine refugium. Morphologically very similar to northern pike and therefore cryptic in the field; its existence as a separate species was entirely unsuspected before genetic analysis. Like all Esox, a solitary ambush predator of vegetated lowland waters.
